coredata.dc:title: Colorimetric determination of cysteine based on inhibition of GSH-Au/Pt NCs as peroxidase mimic
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Jian-Jun
Qiao, Dan
Yang, Shou-Zhi
Weng, Guo-Jun
Zhu, Jian
Zhao, Jun-Wu
coredata.dc:description: In this work, we reported a facile and highly sensitive strategy for colorimetric detection of cysteine (Cys) based on the inhibition of catalytic activity of bimetallic nanoclusters induced by Cys. Glutathione-modified gold-platinum nanoclusters (GSH-Au/Pt NCs) with different Au/Pt molar ratios were prepared via one-pot approach and utilized as peroxidase mimics to catalyze the oxidation of 3,3’,5,5’-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) by H2O2. It has been found that Cys could inhibit the peroxidase-like activity of GSH-Au/Pt NCs efficiently, which leads to a decrease of the absorption intensity of the system at 652 nm with a fading of the blue color. These findings provide a worthy method for visualization and quantitative detection of Cys with different concentrations in the range from 0.5 to 30 μM, and the detection limit is 0.154 μM. Moreover, this method displays a promising application in colorimetric analysis of Cys in urine samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Au/Pt bimetallic nanoclusters
Peroxidase-like activity
Colorimetric assay
Cysteine

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of Cys using GSH-Au/Pt NCs as peroxidase mimic
Peroxidase-mimicking activity inhibition of GSH-Au/Pt NCs by Cys
Mechanism of colorimetric assay of cysteine

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Absorption spectra of different reaction systems, the insets are the corresponding solution photos. (a) Au/Pt NCs-H2O2-TMB solution; (b) Cys-Au/Pt NCs-H2O2-TMB solution; (c) H2O2-TMB solution.
Schematic illustration of the GSH-Au/Pt NCs inhibition-based colorimetric assay for detecting cysteine.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) of (a) Au 4f and (b) Pt 4f spectra of GSH-Au/Pt NCs before adding cysteine, respectively. The XPS spectra of (c) Au 4f and (d) Pt 4f for GSH-Au/Pt NCs after adding cysteine, respectively.
(a) HRTEM image and (b) size dispersion of GSH-Au/Pt NCs in the absence of cysteine. (c) HRTEM image and (d) size dispersion of GSH-Au/Pt NCs in the presence of cysteine.
(a) Absorption spectra and (b) time-dependent absorbance at 652 nm of TMB–H2O2 reaction system catalyzed by different nanoparticles.
Effects of (a) Au/Pt molar ratio, (b) reaction time, (c) temperature and (d) pH on the absorbance of Au/Pt NCs-H2O2-TMB system incubated with cysteine.

(a) The absorption spectra of the Au/Pt NCs-H2O2-TMB system incubated with cysteine at different concentrations, the insets are the corresponding solution photos. (b) The linear relationship between ΔA652 nm and the logarithmic concentration of cysteine.
Comparison of the inhibition effects of various agents on the catalytic activity of GSH-Au/Pt NCs (a) system incubated with interferents (b) system incubated with interferents and Cys.
Comparison of several reported cysteine analytical methods.
Results for the detection of cysteine in urine samples and serum samples.

head.ce:title: Colorimetric determination of cysteine based on inhibition of GSH-Au/Pt NCs as peroxidase mimic

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: The Key Laboratory of Biomedical Information Engineering of Ministry of Education, School of Life Science and Technology, Xi’an Jiaotong University, Xi’an 710049, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: The Key Laboratory of Biomedical Information Engineering of Ministry of Education
School of Life Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: The Key Laboratory of Biomedical Information Engineering of Ministry of Education, School of Life Science and Technology, Xi’an Jiaotong University, Xi’an 710049, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: School of Life Science and Technology, Xi’an Jiaotong University, Xi’an 710049, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Life Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, we reported a facile and highly sensitive strategy for colorimetric detection of cysteine (Cys) based on the inhibition of catalytic activity of bimetallic nanoclusters induced by Cys. Glutathione-modified gold-platinum nanoclusters (GSH-Au/Pt NCs) with different Au/Pt molar ratios were prepared via one-pot approach and utilized as peroxidase mimics to catalyze the oxidation of 3,3’,5,5’-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) by H2O2. It has been found that Cys could inhibit the peroxidase-like activity of GSH-Au/Pt NCs efficiently, which leads to a decrease of the absorption intensity of the system at 652 nm with a fading of the blue color. These findings provide a worthy method for visualization and quantitative detection of Cys with different concentrations in the range from 0.5 to 30 μM, and the detection limit is 0.154 μM. Moreover, this method displays a promising application in colorimetric analysis of Cys in urine samples.

ce:section.ce:para: Cysteine(Cys)is a type of non-essential amino acids containing a thiol group, which plays a pivotal role in the basic living processes, such as eliminating free radicals in the body, removing toxins and drugs, participating in the reduction process of cells and so on [1,2]. Moreover, the concentration of Cys is closely associated with various diseases, for example, Cys deficiency may lead to liver damage, growth retardation and heart diseases [3]. Therefore, it is very important to develop a facile and feasible method for accurate detection of Cys in body fluids [4]. So far, numerous traditional approaches, including electrochemistry [5], high-performance liquid chromatography [6], fluorescent method [7] and so on, have been developed to detect Cys in biological fluids. Among them, a colorimetric technology has aroused considerable attention because of the advantages of high sensitivity, low cost, rapid and convenient process [8–10]. It should be noted that obvious color changes can be observed in the reaction system based on colorimetric assay, allowing for visual readout by the naked eye and quantitative analysis with a UV–vis spectroscopy [11]. Hence, colorimetric method has a great potential in specific detection of Cys. However, the establishment of colorimetric probes with high sensitivity is still a challenge.

Recently, noble metal nanoclusters (NCs) comprising a few to dozens of atoms have received increasing attention owing to their unique structures and distinct properties [12]. For example, Zahra et al.[13] established a method for detecting campylobacterjejuni based on its inhibition of Au/Ag nanocluster fluorescence emission. Except for fluorescence effect, the ultra-small nanoclusters have also been found to possess highly peroxidase-like activity because of their large surface-to-volume ratio, and they have been widely used in the field of biomedical diagnosis as new artificial enzyme mimics [14]. For instance, Zahra et al.[15,16] detected the amylase by using its inhibition ability to the peroxidase-like activity of starch-supported Cu/Au nanocluster, and they also used the peroxidase-like activity of Au/Pt nanoclusters to trace lead ions in another work. Nowadays, it is a current research interest to develop the nanomaterial-based enzyme mimics that exhibit many advantages over natural enzymes, such as higher tunability in enzyme-like activities, better stability and easier fabrication process [9,17,18]. These favorable features make nanoclusters have a great potential in practical applications. Up to now, lots of analytical methods are created from the similar strategy by measuring H2O2 involving in corresponding oxidase reactions, which limits their applications [19]. Thus, to expand their applications, it is meaningful to explore new analytical strategies with the help of catalytic activity.

According to current literatures, the catalytic activity of bimetallic nanoclusters would be affected by many factors including size, component and the surface ligand of nanoclusters [20,21]. For instance, Zheng et al.[22] demonstrated that bimetallic Ag-Pt NCs showed much higher enzyme-mimicking activity than monometallic Ag NCs or Pt NCs, which was attributed to the synergistic effects of these two kinds of atoms. In addition, the catalytic activity of many bimetallic nanoclusters was found to be size-dependent, recognized as “size effect”[23]. In general, the catalytic activity of bimetallic nanoclusters increases with the reducing of size [24]. Chen et al.[23] compared the catalytic activity of different Pt-based nanomaterials by using ABTS as peroxide substrate, the trend was lysozyme stabilized Pt NCs > Pt NPs (5 nm) > Pt NPs (30 nm). Therefore, we seek to synthesize ultra-small bimetallic nanoclusters with highly efficient catalytic activity, and establish a novel colorimetric method. More interestingly, some substances combined to the surface of nanoclusters could enhance peroxidase activity of nanoclusters, whereas others could show inhibition effect [25]. On the basis of these findings, using their different effects on the catalytic performance of metal nanoclusters, quantitative detection of various targets can be achieved.

In this paper, a simple and reliable sensing platform was proposed for colorimetric detection of Cys based on its inhibition effect on the peroxidase mimicking activity of glutathione-stabilized gold-platinum nanoclusters (GSH-Au/Pt NCs). The GSH-Au/Pt NCs possess peroxidase mimetic enzyme activity due to Pt doping-induced improvement of their catalytic properties, which can catalyze the oxidation of chromogenic substrate TMB by H2O2 [26], producing a blue-colored derivative oxidized TMB (ox-TMB)[27]. It is found that the catalytic activity of GSH-Au/Pt NCs could be effectively inhibited after adding Cys into the reaction system, which leads to a decrease in absorbance and a blue color fading, giving the visual signal readout. And the intensity of absorption peak at 652 nm exhibits a strong dependence on Cys concentrations, based on which a colorimetric method with high sensitivity for quantitative evaluation and visualization of Cys level is developed. This system shows high accuracy and good precision, which is a promising biosensor to be used for point-of-care of Cys in biomedical diagnosis.

Chemicals were used as received without further purification. Chloroauric acid (HAuCl4), hexachloroplatinic acid (H2PtCl6), 30% H2O2 and 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine were bought from Sigma–Aldrich. Glutathione (GSH), cysteine (Cys), histidine (His) and other reagents were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd, China. Aqueous solutions were prepared by deionized water purified by a Milli-Q system (18.2 MΩ·cm, Millipore, USA).

A UV-3600 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan) was used to measure the UV–vis absorption spectra recorded from 450 to 1200 nm at room temperature. Absorbance of the ox-TMB at 652 nm was monitored for quantitative analysis. The size and morphology of the synthesized nanoclusters were characterized by a JEM-F200 instrument (JEOL, Japan) operating at 200 kV. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis was performed on an ESCALAB Xi+ (Thermo Fisher Scientific, America).

Glutathione-modified gold-platinum bimetallic nanoclusters (GSH-Au/Pt NCs) were prepared by referencing reported literature with slight modification [28]. In a typical synthesis, 2 mL of HAuCl4 solution (4 mM) was added to 2 mL of H2PtCl6 solutions with different concentrations at room temperature, followed by quickly injecting 2 mL of fresh GSH solution (6 mM) into the above mixture. Then the resulting solution was refluxed for 6.5 h under vigorous stirring at 90 °C. The obtained yellow-colored GSH-Au/Pt NCs solution was stored at 4℃ in the dark prior to use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Cys using GSH-Au/Pt NCs as peroxidase mimic
ce:section.ce:para: A typical colorimetric detection of Cys was performed as following steps. Firstly, 100 μL of GSH-Au/Pt NCs was introduced into 500 μL of 0.2 M NaAc-HAc buffer at pH = 4, followed by adding 1 mL of various concentrations of Cys solutions (ranging from 0 to 30 μM). Secondly, all the mixed solutions were incubated for 15 min in a water bath at 35 °C. Finally, 200 μL of H2O2 (0.1 M)、200 μL of TMB (2.5 mM) were added successively into the above solution. After 10 min-reaction at 35 °C, the absorbance signals at 652 nm (A652 nm) of the resultant solutions were recorded by UV–vis spectroscopy.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Peroxidase-mimicking activity inhibition of GSH-Au/Pt NCs by Cys
ce:section.ce:para: TMB, a typical chromogenic substrate of peroxidase, was utilized to evaluate the catalytic activity of GSH-Au/Pt NCs [29]. As can be seen in spectra (a) and (c) of Fig. 1 , in the coexistence of H2O2, the oxidation process of colorless TMB can be catalyzed by GSH-Au/Pt NCs to generate blue derivative ox-TMB, with a strong absorption peak appeared at 652 nm [30]. Since GSH-Au/Pt NCs have high affinity for small molecules owing to their high surface-to-volume ratios caused by the ultra-small size and abundant surface binding sites, it is beneficial for nanoclusters full contact with H2O2 molecules, which promotes the O-O bond of H2O2 broken into two ·OH radicals with high oxidation, leading to TMB easily be oxidized by H2O2 [31,32]. However, when a certain amount of Cys was introduced into the system, a sharp decline of the absorbance at 652 nm was observed and the visible color was weakened (from blue-green to colorless), suggesting that Cys can dramatically inhibited the catalytic activity of GSH-Au/Pt NCs. Meanwhile, the absorption intensity at 652 nm indirectly reflects the content of Cys in the system. On the basis of above phenomenon, a colorimetric method for quantitative detection of Cys was designed by monitoring the changes of absorbance at 652 nm. The working principle of the sensing system toward the target Cys is illustrated in Fig. 2 . This mechanism of cysteine determination has also been reported in the ref.[31,33].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of colorimetric assay of cysteine
ce:section.ce:para: To further understand the interactions between GSH-Au/Pt NCs and Cys, XPS was carried out to analyze the chemical states of both Au and Pt elements in the GSH-Au/Pt NCs with and without the treatment of Cys. As shown in Fig. 3 (a) and 3(b), The Au 4f peak is a doublet composed of Au 4f7/2 and Au 4f5/2 peaks. The binding energy of Au 4f is located at 83.52 (56.51%) and 87.20 eV (43.49%), corresponding to Au0 and Au+, respectively [34,35]. Similarly, the binding energy of Pt 4f can be deconstructed into Pt0 and Pt2+ components with binding energies placed of 71.91 (90.62%) and 73.10 eV (9.38%), respectively [36,37]. It can be seen in Fig. 3(c) and 3(d) that the content of Pt0 increases from 90.62% to 92.82% while the Au specie remains unchanged after adding Cys, indicating that the molar fraction of Pt0 and Pt2+ in Pt species has already been changed. This is mainly because of the lower electrochemical potentials of Au+ than Pt2+, resulting in the harder reduction Au+ than that of Pt2+ by Cys [31]. On the other hand, Cys can be attached to the surface of GSH-Au/Pt NCs through Au-S bond to prevent the interaction between GSH-Au/Pt NCs and H2O2, resulting in the amount of ·OH radicals decreasing and the process of TMB oxidation suppressed [38].

TEM analysis was performed to observe the changes in the morphology and size of GSH-Au/Pt NCs after the addition of Cys. The TEM images and size distributions of GSH-Au/Pt NCs in the absence and presence of Cys were displayed in Fig. 4 (a-d). One can find that the diameter of nanoclusters increases from 2.08 nm to 4.15 nm, the surface-to-volume ratios is decreased, which leads to much weaker catalytic activity of GSH-Au/Pt NCs. These TEM data of GSH-Au/Pt NCs demonstrate that the size of nanoclusters is enlarged after the Pt atoms deposit on the surface of GSH-Au/Pt NCs [39,40].

In order to achieve the most sensitive responses of the GSH-Au/Pt NCs to Cys, a series of experimental conditions were optimized including the molar ratio of Au/Pt, incubation time, reaction temperature and pH of buffer. In this work, the absorbance change at 652 nm (△A652 nm (△A652 nm = A0-A) is exploited as a criterion to evaluate the sensing ability, where A0 and A are the absorbance values at 652 nm without and with the addition of Cys, respectively.

As shown in Fig. 5 (a) and (b), we are surprised to find that Pt can obviously promote the peroxidase-like activity of Au NCs for TMB oxidation in the presence of H2O2. Compared to pure Pt NCs and Au NCs, an obvious increase of the absorption intensity at 652 nm is observed and the reaction of TMB with H2O2 is greatly accelerated after addition of Pt atoms. The catalytic activity of Au NCs could be greatly enhanced by simple Pt doping, which is due to the unique electronic structure and high catalytic performance of Pt [22,28,41]. On the other hand, improved activity of GSH-Au/Pt NCs is mainly owing to the synergistic effect between Au and Pt atoms. As reported previously, the catalytic efficiency of Pt catalysts with spaced structure is higher over Pt catalysts with continuous structure. The presence of Au contributes to form Pt catalysts with interval arrangement structure, resulting in catalytic activity of bimetallic GSH-Au/Pt NCs improving. Furthermore, Au can suppress the formation of by-products, preventing catalyst deactivation and improving the service life of the catalyst.

Since the molar ratio of Au/Pt was an important factor in the optical properties of bimetallic nanoclusters, the effect of it on the peroxidase-like activity of GSH-Au/Pt NCs was first investigated by adjusting the amounts of HAuCl4 and H2PtCl6 in the reaction system. As shown in Fig. 6 (d), for GSH-Au/Pt NCs that owns different Au/Pt molar ratios, the variation trend of the changes of absorbance at 652 nm illustrates a dramatic promotion with the doping extent of Pt increasing, and the △A652 nm yields a maximum value at the molar ratio of Au/Pt of 5. Previous researches have demonstrated that the particle size gradually decreases as the content of Pt increasing, therefore, the catalytic activity of GSH-Au/Pt NCs is improved, the inhibition on the catalytic activity of GSH-Au/Pt NCs by Cys is enhanced with the decrease of the Au/Pt molar ratios [41]. In contrast, metallic nanoclusters prepared at high H2PtCl6 concentrations tend to be aggregated producing much bigger size. The specific surface area of nanoclusters would be reduced due to the “size effect”, which results in the low catalytic activity [31].

Secondly, the time-dependent absorbance at 652 nm of TMB–H2O2 reaction system was investigated and the results were displayed in Fig. 6(b). It can be observed that the △A652 nm increases as the reaction time increasing, and the maximum value of △A652 nm is received at 15 min, followed by the change of absorbance starting to decrease. Hence, the reaction time of 15 min is selected as the optimal time.

Thirdly, the influence of reaction temperature, ranging from 20 to 40℃, on the sensing performance was studied. Temperature mainly affects the reaction rate, and the low temperature usually leads to low reaction rate causing the inhibition effect on the peroxidase-like activity of GSH-Au/Pt NCs becomes weaker. As indicated in Fig. 6(c), the △A652 nm gradually increases with the increment of temperature and it is sharply decreased beyond 35℃, which is probably attributed to negative effect on the stability of both H2O2 and ox-TMB. Accordingly, the optimal reaction temperature is 35℃.

Fig. 6(d) showed the effect of different pH value of solution from 3.6 to 5.6 on the △A652 nm. It can be seen that the △A652 nm reaches a maximum at pH = 4.0, and then drops when the pH increases further, in which H2O2 tends to decompose into H2O and O2 rather than ·OH radicals resulting in a decline on ox-TMB under the higher pH condition [42]. Meanwhile, the reaction would be inhibited by acid terminator where the pH value is lower than 4.0[43]. Therefore, the pH of 4.0 is adopted as the optimal pH value.

Under the optimized conditions, the analytical performance of the developed colorimetric protocol was further explored by using GSH-Au/Pt NCs to accelerate the H2O2-TMB colorimetric reaction after incubation with different amount of Cys. As depicted in Fig. 7 (a), the absorbance of the oxidized TMB (ox-TMB) at 652 nm gradually decreases from 0.433 to 0.086 with raising Cys concentration from 0.5 to 30 μM, and distinct color changes of ox-TMB solutions from deep blue to colourless are observed with naked eye, indicating that the catalytic activity of GSH-Au/Pt NCs could be obviously inhibited by Cys. Additionally, it can be seen from Fig. 7(b) that the absorbance change (ΔA652 nm) is linear to the logarithmic Cys concentration in the range from 0.5 to 30 μM, and the calibration curve could be expressed as y = 1.12274–0.17517x with a correlation coefficient of R2 = 0.9929. The corresponding limit of detection (LOD) is about 0.154 μM, which is calculated by the formula of 3σ/k with a signal-to-noise ratio (S/N) of 3, where σ represents the standard deviation of blank measurements, and k is the slope of working curve.

To demonstrate the superiority of the proposed Au/Pt NCs-H2O2-TMB sensing system in Cys determination, a performance comparison between the present method and other reported ways for Cys detection was listed in Table 1 . It is clear that this sensing system has a lower LOD, which is more sensitive than previous reported assays. This high sensitivity is attributed to the enhanced peroxidase-like activity of GSH-Au/Pt NCs upon introduction of Pt. The result suggests that this developed method based on the inhibition of the catalytic activity of GSH-Au/Pt NCs by Cys provides a easy, sensitive and specific strategy for detection of Cys.

For a detection device, one of the most essential parameter is the target selectivity. To evaluate the selectivity, the responses of the method towards different interferers, including leucine (Leu), phenylalanine (Phe), methionine (Met), homocysteine (Hcy), ascorbic acid and so on, were studied by monitoring the change of the absorbance at 652 nm. Specially, the concentrations of Cys, GSH and Hcy were set as 3 × 10-5 M and the concentrations of ascorbic acid was set as 6 × 10-5 M, while the concentrations of the rest of agents were 3 × 10-4 M, and all experiments were performed under the same conditions. As displayed in Fig. 8 , the addition of biothiols causes obvious changes in the absorption at 652 nm, significantly inhibiting the peroxidase-like activity of GSH-Au/Pt NCs while these potential interfering substances only have a slight effect on the inhibition effect of GSH-Au/Pt NCs. It can also be found that when Cys mixed with other amino acids at higher concentrations, the value of the absorption peak is not affected (Fig. 8b), indicating our sensing system has excellent selectivity towards Cys even with the coexistence of these interfering substances, which is caused by the drastic interaction between the thiol group of the cysteine and Pt to regulate the catalytic behavior of GSH-Au/Pt NCs. During the experiment, we found the nanozyme was more sensitive to cysteine than GSH and Hcy. Although these three amino acids all own -SH groups, they actually have different structure of the whole molecule. Besides, there are also other previous reports which have found similar phenomenon. Chen et al. found that bovine serum albumin-confined silver nanoclusters are more sensitive to cysteine than GSH and Hcy although they are all biothiols [1]. The same result also was showed in the report of Nebu et al., Li et al., Li et al. and so on [47,48,49,50]. At the same time, considering the Cys concentration is markedly higher than that of GSH and Hcy in human urine, the interference of them could be ignored [46,51]. Therefore, this assay developed here offers a high selectivity for quantitative analysis of Cys in biological environments.

To examine the practical feasibility of our sensing system for colorimetric detection of Cys, spiked-recovery experiments were performed by using artificial urine and serum. We then determined the content of Cys in samples after adding different concentrations of Cys into urine and serum samples (the final concentrations of real samples are 1, 2.5 and 10 μM). As can be seen in Table 2 , the recoveries of the proposed method are ranged from 97.86% to 102.42% and 96.50% to 101.42% respectively, and the relative standard deviation (RSD) of this probe is within the range of 0.231%-2.21% with three repeated measurements. These results demonstrate that our approach is accurate and reliable for Cys detection in complex biological samples.

In summary, we efficiently fabricated the gold-platinum bimetallic nanoclusters using glutathione as template. The synthetized bimetallic nanoclusters have high peroxidase-like activity, which could be adjusted by controlling the molar ratio of Au/Pt. By combining with the catalytic activity of GSH-Au/Pt NCs and the inhibition of Cys, a facile colorimetric method for ultrasensitive detection of Cys was established. Under the optimal conditions, good linearity was observed between the △A652 nm values and the logarithmic concentration of Cys from 0.5 to 30 μM, with a limit of detection as low as 0.154 μM. Furthermore, this presented approach endows Cys sensing with operation simplicity, fast response, high sensitive and good precision. What’s more, it provides a direct readout of the results with the naked eye, which appears to be a promising tool for clinical diagnosis applications.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China under grant No. 61675162. We also would like to thank the Zhejiang Province Basic Public Welfare Research Project (LGF20H180017) for their support in this research.
